frameworks.name: higher-order theory of consciousness
frameworks.one_line: subjective visibility reports reflect a higher-order representation dissociable from sensory evidence

frameworks.name: signal-detection theory
frameworks.one_line: objective discriminability vs subjective visibility quantified across matched stimulus strengths

frameworks.name: subjective inflation
frameworks.one_line: inattention inflates visibility beyond what sensory accuracy predicts, extended past threshold vision

core_question: Does spatial attention dissociate subjective visibility reports from objective orientation-discrimination performance across stimulus strengths and types?
key_assumptions: objective and subjective measures can be quantitatively related when discriminability is equated
area-under-the-curve over stimulus strength provides a valid index of inflation
spatial cueing manipulates attention without changing the physical stimulus
manipulations: stimulus strength across threshold and suprathreshold levels
key_findings: subjective visibility was higher for unattended than attended stimuli at matched orientation discriminability
the dissociation was strong and consistent across all three preregistered experiments
inflation appeared across threshold and suprathreshold stimulus strengths, not only at threshold
interpretations: inattention robustly inflates subjective visibility reports independent of sensory accuracy
subjective inflation is not confined to threshold vision, supporting higher-order accounts of awareness
